text:
Admission Date:  2007-12-26     Discharge Date:  2012-06-08

Date of Birth:   1986-12-22     Sex:  M

Service:  Cardiothoracic Surgery

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient's past medical
history is significant for coronary artery disease, status
post myocardial infarction in 1983, and status post
percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty to his mid
left anterior descending artery and first diagonal in 1991-08-07, hypertension, non-insulin-dependent diabetes
mellitus, psoriatic arthritis, hemorrhoids, and a history of
a bowel infection in 1998-01-27.

This is a 47-year-old white male who presented to Ferrell
Emergency Room after experiencing crushing substernal chest
pain while walking at 2 o'clock in the afternoon on the day
of admission (which was 2007-12-26).  The patient's
pain did improve with rest, and in the Emergency Room the
pain was completely resolved with a nitroglycerin drip.

The patient was then transferred to Hale County Hospital pain free on arrival.  An electrocardiogram at
that time showed less than 1-mm ST elevations in V2 and V3.

The patient underwent cardiac catheterization on 1998-01-30 which revealed 2-vessel coronary artery disease, 70%
stenosis of the mid right coronary artery, 99% stenosis of
the first diagonal, and 100% stenosis of the second obtuse
marginal.  The patient was then referred to the
Cardiothoracic Surgery Service for coronary artery bypass
graft surgery.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:  The patient's medications on
admission were atenolol 100 mg p.o. q.d., Univasc 15 mg p.o.
q.d., Plavix 75 mg p.o. q.d., aspirin 325 mg p.o. q.d.,
Lipitor 40 mg p.o. q.d., Enbrel every week, and metformin
p.o. b.i.d. (dose unknown).

ALLERGIES:  The patient has no known drug allergies.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  The patient has a 40-pack-year tobacco
history; however, he quit three years ago.

HOSPITAL COURSE:   The patient underwent coronary artery
bypass graft times five on 1992-12-24 with a left
internal mammary artery to the left anterior descending
artery, saphenous vein graft to the diagonal, saphenous vein
graft to the posterior descending artery, and the left radial
artery to the ramus intermedius and obtuse marginal
sequentially.

The patient was transferred to the Coronary Care Unit
Recovery Unit in stable condition in a normal sinus rhythm at
80 beats per minute, on 0.5 mcg/kg per minute of
nitroglycerin, 1 mcg/kg per minute of Neo-Synephrine,
propofol, and an insulin drip at 4 units per hour.  The
patient was extubated early the following morning (at 2
o'clock in the morning) without complications.

On postoperative day one, the patient had a temperature
maximum of 101.1; still on insulin, Neo-Synephrine, and
nitroglycerin drips.  In sinus tachycardia at 118.
Otherwise, vital signs were stable.  White blood cell count
was 14.2, hematocrit was 30.6, platelet count was 255.
Sodium was 139, potassium was 4.4,  blood urea nitrogen was
8, creatinine was 0.9, and blood glucose was 131.

On physical examination; the patient was awake, alert, and
oriented with an otherwise normal examination.  The plan was
to continue the nitroglycerin drip for the radial artery
harvest and to wean the Neo-Synephrine.

On postoperative day two, the patient was still with a
low-grade temperature with a temperature maximum of 100.4.
The patient was still in sinus tachycardia at 110.
Otherwise, vital signs were stable.  On 3 liters nasal
cannula with an oxygen saturation of 93%.  Off of the
Neo-Synephrine; however, still on an insulin drip.  White
blood cell count was 10.1, hematocrit was 25.2,, and a
platelet count of 190.  Sodium was 137, potassium was 5.1,
blood urea nitrogen was 7, creatinine was 0.9, and a glucose
of 103.  The patient's physical examination remained
unchanged, and the plan was to continue to administer
Neo-Synephrine intermittently until weaned off; at which
point the patient would then receive Lopressor.

The patient was transferred to the floor on postoperative day
two in stable condition.

On postoperative day three, the patient only had complaints
of incisional pain with a temperature maximum of 100.3;
however, currently at that point was afebrile, in sinus
rhythm at 92, blood pressure was 139/82, saturating 94% on
room air.  On physical examination, the patient had sparse
crackles.  Otherwise, the examination was benign, and the
plan was to remove the pacing wires and for possible
discharge home planning.

The patient had [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Chavez Diabetes consultation on
postoperative day three; at which time they recommended to
discontinue his regular insulin, start Glucophage 500 mg
b.i.d. for five days, then 1000 mg in the morning and 500 mg
in the evening, then 1000 mg b.i.d. (to be taken with meals
at breakfast and supper time).  To also add Amaryl 2 mg p.o.
q.d. with breakfast.  They also highly recommended that the
patient see a dietician and exercise physiologist at Nunez,
and they gave a telephone number for him to schedule in order
to attend classes.  They also reinforced the importance of an
annual eye examination and recommended microalbumin levels to
be monitored as an outpatient and lipids to be followed as
well.

On postoperative day four, the patient was without
complaints.  He was using his incentive spirometry with less
coughing.  The patient with a low-grade temperature with a
temperature maximum of 100.7, temperature current was 100.4,
in sinus rhythm at 94.  Vital signs were stable.

On physical examination, the patient with bilateral crackles;
however, today with the sternal incision having minimal
drainage and slight erythema at the upper pole of the
incision; however, the lower leg extremity incision was
clean, dry, and intact.  The plan was to keep the patient
overnight and for possible discharge in the morning; assuming
the sternal drainage had improved.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:  The date of anticipated discharge was
2012-06-08.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:  (The discharge medications the
patient was anticipated to go home on were)
 1.  Metoprolol 75 mg p.o. b.i.d.
 2.  Amaryl 2 mg p.o. q.d.
 3.  Metformin (dosage to be given on the day of discharge).
 4.  Imdur 60 mg p.o. q.d.
 5.  Atorvastatin 40 mg p.o. q.d.
 6.  Percocet one to two tablets p.o. q.4-6h. as needed (for
pain).
 7.  Colace 100 mg p.o. b.i.d. as needed (for constipation).
 8.  Ranitidine 150 mg p.o. b.i.d.
 9.  Lasix 20 mg p.o. b.i.d. (for 10 days).
10.  Potassium chloride 20 mEq p.o. b.i.d. (for 10 days).

DISCHARGE PLAN:  The plan was for the patient to follow up
with Dr. Collins in four weeks.

CONDITION AT DISCHARGE:  The patient's condition on discharge
was stable.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Coronary artery disease.
2.  Status post coronary artery bypass graft times five.





                          [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) 275**] Foley, M.D.  59354

Dictated By:Sanchez
